symptom: Low suction pressure, frost on suction line, weak airflow at the supply registers, and a higher-than-normal temperature drop across the coil. Apprentice may misread this as low refrigerant charge.
diagnosis: Restricted airflow across the indoor coil — usually a dirty filter, dirty evaporator, collapsed flex duct, or closed dampers. The starved coil cannot pick up enough heat from the air, so suction pressure drops and the line cold-soaks into a frost pattern that looks identical to a low-charge call.
procedure: Check the filter before touching the gauges — a clogged filter is the single most common cause.
Inspect the evaporator coil for dust mat or biological growth; clean if dirty.
Measure static pressure across the air handler with a manometer; compare to design (typically 0.5 in. WC).
Verify blower speed tap matches the equipment selection.
Inspect ductwork for crushed flex, closed dampers, or undersized returns.
Re-measure superheat and suction pressure after airflow is restored before deciding charge is wrong.
safety_notes: A new tech who adds refrigerant to a restricted-airflow system will overcharge it, then flood the compressor on liquid return.
Do not run the system with a coil frozen solid — let it thaw fully before re-checking pressures.
Wear a dust mask when cleaning a heavily fouled evaporator coil.
